title: The Barren Tree
subtitle: The Lord seeks fruit on the fig tree, and finds none — yet stays the axe one more year

stillness: Be still, and let the noise of the world fall away.
Bring before you the patience of God, and the negligence of man.
Sit quietly beneath the tree that bears no fruit.
gospel: There were some present who told Jesus
of the Galileans whose blood Pilate had mingled with their sacrifices.
Do you think these Galileans were sinners above all the others,
No, I say to you; but unless you repent,
Or those eighteen, on whom the tower of Siloam fell and slew them —
do you think they were guilty above all who dwell in Jerusalem?
No; but unless you do penance, you shall all perish.
A man had a fig tree planted in his vineyard,
and he came seeking fruit on it, and found none.
Cut it down then; why does it even cumber the ground?
But the keeper answered: Lord, let it alone this year also,
until I dig about it and cast on dung;
and if it bear fruit — well; but if not, cut it down.
behold: Behold the fig tree, planted in the vineyard, taking up the ground to no purpose.
Three years the lord of the vineyard has come, seeking fruit, and has found none.
Three years: the edicts of the Law, the testimony of the Prophets, the grace of the flashing Gospel.
God sought the fruit of good works, and found it — but in very few, who against so great a multitude were counted as nothing.
Hear the sentence: Cut it down then; why does it even cumber the ground?
For each one, in his own measure, if he shows not the fruit of good working, like a barren tree cumbers the ground.
But the keeper pleads: Lord, let it alone this year also — until I dig about it and cast on dung.
To dig around it is to teach the lowliness of penance and of patience; for the trench is humble, lowered earth.
The dung is filth: the remembrance of sins — and yet it gives fruit.
For what is filthier than dung? And yet, if you use it well, what is more fruitful?
From the dung the tree revives unto fruit, because from the consideration of its sin it raises itself again to good works.
And this, too, is your soul: a tree, a vineyard, a garden, the field of Him who must till it, that it may bear fruit.
But — alas! — in many who take up the ground to no purpose, no fruit is found.
consider: The Lord does not deny that those slain were sinners, and that for this they perished.
Yet he does not call them worse than those who did not suffer so.
For such a death is no sufficient proof that they sinned more grievously than all the rest.
Often God punishes the lighter sins in this present life,
and defers the graver ones, to be punished more grievously after this life.
They were stricken also for the terror of others — for men are moved to repentance by the sudden and dreadful death of others.
Penance alone delivers from the danger: it is the second plank after shipwreck.
See here how great is the patience of God, and how great the negligence of man.
Three years He waited, warned, visited — and still complains He found no fruit:
for the minds of certain wicked men neither the inbreathed law of nature corrects,
nor the commandments instruct, nor the miracles of His Incarnation convert.
With fear must this be heard: Cut it down then; why does it even cumber the ground?
The digging is the recognizing of sins; the sinner covers his sins, but by digging he lays his conscience bare.
Use the filth well, and it is fruitful: from the consideration of sin, rise again to good works.
prayer: grant me to do the fruit of good works fervently,
and to persevere in them without ceasing,
lest, being found without fruit, I deserve to be cut down and cast into the fire.
that I be not bent downward through sin,
nor, curved away from the uprightness of mind,
think upon and love things earthly and passing,
but lift the eyes of my mind to the contemplation of things heavenly,
and seek the riches of eternal blessedness.
O Lord, look upon me through Your mercy;
heal me through the remission of sin;
touch me through the sorrow of making amends;
raise me to the things above through the fervor of love.

latinJewel.latin: Quid enim stercore sordidius? Et tamen si eo bene utaris, quid fructuosius?
latinJewel.english: For what is filthier than dung? And yet, if you use it well, what is more fruitful?
